title: Concrete lvalue-reference parameters are const-default
summary: A concrete lvalue reference without mut is read-only; writable references carry mut, matching the deduced-reference visual rule.
details: const Foo& input is presented as input: Foo&.
Foo& output is presented as output: mut Foo&.
This decision changes only lvalue-reference parameter presentation, including lambda parameters.
Pointer pointee constness remains written after the colon, and pointer-object constness remains a const binding prefix.
Reference sigils bind tightly to the type regardless of the source project's east/west declarator spacing.
rationale: The frontend's central model is const-by-default and exception-forward; concrete references should not be the lone common binding form that repeats the default.
The real-render comparison made the read-only versus mut contrast legible across local, CUDA, ImGui, LLVM, and GCC styles.
Keeping pointer axes explicit avoids pretending that the simpler reference rule answers the more complex pointer problem.
alternatives: Keep written const on concrete references while deduced references remain compact; this was the previous asymmetric production rule.
Hide pointer pointee const at the same time; rejected as out of scope because no complete unambiguous pointer notation was selected.

provenance.note: The selected option is the production default, not only an experimental profile.

evidence.claim: Captures the selected choice, time, and exact catalog hash shown to the owner.
Both alternatives are reproducible and the selected default is isolated from explicit comparison buffers.

questions: Which reference-parameter alternative did the owner choose?
Why is const Foo& shown without const?
Did the reference decision also hide pointer constness?

last_verified.method: Selection provenance, production code, and post-selection render assertions agree.
